title: Fire
summary: The first technology — controlled combustion that cooked food and expanded the human brain, enabled migration into cold climates, powered metallurgy, drove industrial civilization, and now reshapes the atmosphere through the carbon it releases
hook: Fire is the first technology. Every other human achievement — cooking, ceramics, metallurgy, the steam engine, and the fossil fuel economy — descends from the moment a hominid learned to control it. The controlled use of fire may have made us human in the most literal sense: cooking food releases more calories per bite than raw food, and that surplus funded the metabolically expensive brain that distinguishes Homo sapiens from every other species. The feedback from that first fire is still running — visible now as a rising line on the Keeling Curve, the continuous record of atmospheric CO₂ measured at Mauna Loa since 1958.

beginner.theFoundation:
Fire is the oldest human technology. It predates every other tool by hundreds of thousands of years. Before fire, humans ate raw food and could only survive in warm climates. After fire, humans could cook food, stay warm through cold winters, see in darkness, and keep large predators away.

But fire did not just change what humans could do. It changed what humans were. Cooking breaks down plant cells and releases nutrients that raw food locks away. More calories per meal meant more energy available for the brain. The human brain is extraordinarily expensive — it uses a fifth of the body's energy despite being only a fortieth of its weight. Fire, by making food more nutritious, helped pay for it.
beginner.howItArrived:
Nobody knows exactly when humans first controlled fire. The oldest clear evidence is roughly 400,000 years ago. Signs of fire use possibly a million years old have been found in South Africa, though these are disputed. What is clear is that early humans were using fire long before Homo sapiens existed.

At first, fire was probably captured from natural sources — lightning strikes, volcanic vents, sparks from rock falls. Keeping that fire alive required careful tending and organization. Making fire deliberately — striking flint against iron pyrite, or spinning a wooden drill fast enough to generate heat — came later, perhaps within the last 100,000 years. The ability to make fire on demand, wherever you happened to be, transformed how humans could live and travel.
beginner.whatItReorganized:
Fire reorganized human life in at least four ways. It made more food available from the same plants and animals. It made cold environments survivable. It extended the day past sunset, adding hours for work, conversation, and the exchange of knowledge. And it created the hearth — the first fixed social center around which groups gathered regularly.

Around fires, early humans began to organize themselves differently. Someone had to tend the fire. Rules developed about who ate first, who sat closest to the warmth. The hearth created predictable meeting points. Anthropologists who study contemporary hunter-gatherer societies find that evening fireside conversations are among the most important social activities — for storytelling, conflict resolution, and the transmission of knowledge across generations.
beginner.thePoliticalEconomy:
Control of fire meant control of warmth, cooked food, and light. In most early societies, fire was maintained collectively — letting it go out was a serious failure with consequences for the whole group. In ancient Egypt, Greece, and Rome, sacred fires burned continuously in temples, tended by priests or dedicated attendants. These fires were not just symbolic. They represented the community's relationship to a technology everyone depended on.

When humans learned to smelt metal, fire became the foundation of military and economic power. Whoever controlled the forge — the materials, the fuel, the knowledge — controlled the production of weapons and tools. Blacksmiths occupied an unusual social position: essential to every community but often regarded with suspicion, associated with transformation and heat and the crossing of ordinary boundaries. The forge fire was not ordinary fire. It was concentrated, directed, and productive in ways that made those who controlled it powerful.
beginner.theFeedback:
Fire's most consequential feedback is still running. When humans burn wood, coal, oil, or natural gas, they release carbon that was previously stored — in trees, in ancient organic matter, in underground deposits that formed over hundreds of millions of years. That carbon accumulates in the atmosphere.

The feedback operates across very different timescales. A forest fire releases carbon stored over decades. Burning coal releases carbon stored over hundreds of millions of years. The atmosphere cannot absorb these additions as fast as industrial combustion produces them. The result is a measurable warming of the planet — driven by the same technology that cooked the first human meal, now operating at industrial scale.
beginner.presentAndFuture:
About eighty percent of the world's energy still comes from burning something. The ongoing shift toward wind, solar, and batteries is the first serious attempt in human history to move away from combustion as the primary energy technology. These technologies do not burn. They convert sunlight and wind directly into electricity without releasing stored carbon.

If this transition succeeds, it will represent the most fundamental change in humanity's relationship to energy since hominids first learned to make fire. Every major civilization in human history has been powered by combustion. Internal combustion engines, coal boilers, gas turbines — all are variations on the same basic technology. The transition underway is not a fuel substitution. It is a different way of getting energy from nature.

general.theFoundation:
Richard Wrangham's cooking hypothesis holds that cooking food was the enabling condition for the evolution of Homo sapiens as a distinct species. Modern humans have smaller teeth, jaws, and digestive tracts than earlier hominids — adaptations that only make sense if food is pre-processed before eating. Cooking extracts roughly 30% more calories from carbohydrates and significantly more from proteins. This caloric surplus funded the human brain, which consumes approximately 20% of the body's energy despite being only 2% of its weight.

The hypothesis is supported by the timing of anatomical changes in the human lineage but remains contested. Some researchers argue that the anatomical evidence is compatible with a diet of mechanically processed raw food, and that cooking became important later. What is not contested is that fire and cooking have been central to human life for at least 400,000 years and possibly much longer, and that the nutritional consequences of cooking are profound.
general.howItArrived:
The transition from capturing natural fire to making it deliberately is one of the most consequential technological achievements in human prehistory — and one of the least understood. Natural fire sources provided opportunities for capture, but maintaining and transporting fire requires significant organizational capacity. Percussion fire-making (striking flint against iron pyrite) and friction fire-making (bow drill) require specific materials, techniques, and practice. The earliest evidence of fire-making tools — as opposed to fire use — suggests this transition happened within the last 100,000 years.

The consequences of deliberate fire-making for human dispersal were profound. With the ability to make fire on demand, humans could move into cold environments that previously excluded them. The colonization of high-latitude Europe and Asia required fire for warmth and cooking. The spread of humans into Australia roughly 65,000 years ago was accompanied by evidence of fire use. Deliberate fire-making may have been a prerequisite for the global dispersal of modern humans.
general.whatItReorganized:
Around fires, division of labor became possible at new scales. The rules about fire-sharing — who eats first, who tends the fire, who controls the best spots — may have been among the earliest formalized social norms. Sleep patterns changed: fire's capacity to keep predators away allowed deeper, longer sleep, and some anthropologists have linked this to cognitive development. The hearth was not just a heat source. It was the first social architecture — the physical center around which the first sustained human groups organized themselves.

The anthropologist Polly Wiessner's research on the Ju/'hoansi people of the Kalahari distinguishes between daytime conversation — predominantly practical, focused on immediate tasks — and firelight conversation, which shifts toward storytelling, history, and social relations. This division suggests the hearth created a distinct social and cognitive space that contributed to the development of cultural transmission and shared narrative.
general.thePoliticalEconomy:
The forge fire is the political economy of iron and bronze condensed into a single location. Control of the forge — the technology, the fuel, the materials — determined who had weapons, agricultural tools, and the surplus to maintain armies. Blacksmiths occupied an ambiguous position in many early societies: simultaneously essential and marginal, associated with both the productive and the dangerous.

The political economy of fire shifted fundamentally with coal and the steam engine: the productive fire could now be moved anywhere coal could be delivered, freeing industry from the constraints of forest and charcoal supply. This liberation from geography was one of the steam engine's most important effects — not just more power, but power anywhere, decoupled from wood supply. The geography of industry reorganized accordingly, concentrating where coal was cheapest to mine and transport.
general.theFeedback:
The carbon cycle feedback from combustion operates at two timescales. Forest fires and agricultural burning release carbon stored over decades to centuries. Fossil fuel combustion releases carbon stored over hundreds of millions of years. The natural carbon cycle can absorb moderate additions, but fossil fuel combustion adds carbon at rates faster than natural processes can process.

Atmospheric CO₂ has risen from approximately 280 parts per million in 1750 to over 420 ppm today — a rate faster than anything in the 800,000-year ice core record. The feedback is now operating at civilizational scale: the same fire that made civilization possible is altering the atmospheric conditions under which civilization developed. This is not a metaphor. It is a direct causal chain from the first controlled hearth to the current warming of the planet.
general.presentAndFuture:
The energy transition away from combustion is better understood as a historical break with fire than as a fuel source substitution. Renewable electricity is not a different kind of burning — it is not burning at all. Internal combustion engines, gas turbines, and coal boilers are all variations on the same basic technology: release energy by setting something on fire. Photovoltaic cells and wind turbines are not. This represents the most fundamental change in humanity's energy technology since the first controlled fire.

Whether the transition will succeed at the required speed is the central question of energy governance in the current period. The infrastructure of combustion — pipelines, refineries, power plants, combustion engines — has been built over two centuries and represents trillions of dollars of investment. Replacing it requires not just new technology but new institutions, new supply chains, and new political arrangements. The technical obstacles are largely solved; the political and economic obstacles are the constraint.

advanced.theFoundation: Wrangham's cooking hypothesis carries an implication that is also its most contested claim: if cooked food enabled modern human brain size, then human cognition is a product of technology rather than technology being a product of human cognition. This inverts the standard Enlightenment picture of the tool-using rational animal who uses tools because of his rationality. Hominids may have become increasingly rational because of a specific technology adopted before they had the cognitive complexity we consider distinctively human. The evidence is primarily anatomical — the timing of digestive tract reduction correlates with the archaeological fire record. The claim is influential and plausible; it is not definitively established.
advanced.howItArrived: The debate about when fire control began has implications for what we understand Homo erectus to have been. If fire control began 1–1.5 million years ago, then the species using fire was not cognitively modern — meaning fire use does not require the planning and social transmission we associate with complex technology. If fire making arrived within the last 100,000 years, then the technology presupposes cognitive modernity. The evidence is genuinely ambiguous and each position carries significant implications for how technology and cognition relate.
advanced.whatItReorganized: Michael Tomasello's work on shared intentionality suggests fire may have been not just a material technology but a social technology: the first shared attentional focus that coordinated human groups around something that was not immediately present food or a predator. If fire-centered arrangements created the first settings in which extended groups could coordinate around a shared object and transmit knowledge across generations, they may have been the enabling context for cumulative cultural evolution — the distinctively human capacity to build on previous generations' achievements rather than starting fresh.
advanced.thePoliticalEconomy: Every major shift in productive fire — from wood to charcoal, charcoal to coal, coal to oil — reorganized not just energy production but labor relations, geographic distribution of industry, and geopolitical power. The transition from firewood to coal moved industry from rural forests to urban coal fields. The transition from coal to oil moved geopolitical power from European coal states to Middle Eastern oil states. Each transition was determined by specific combinations of resource availability, labor relations, and state capacity rather than simply by the energy qualities of the fuel. The transition away from combustion will be determined by the same combination of factors.
advanced.theFeedback:
The Keeling Curve — continuous measurement of atmospheric CO₂ at Mauna Loa Observatory since 1958 — is the most important data series in the history of fire's feedback. CO₂ was 315 ppm when measurement began, passed 400 ppm in 2016, and exceeds 420 ppm today. This rate has no precedent in the 800,000-year ice core record. The species whose cognition fire helped produce is now measuring, in real time, how the cumulative output of that same combustion technology is altering the atmosphere that made complex life possible.

The confrontation the evidence does not settle: two incompatible framings dominate serious analysis of the energy transition. The technological optimist account holds that renewable energy is now cheaper than fossil fuels in most markets; the transition is economically inevitable; the question is only pace. The political economy account holds that existing fossil fuel infrastructure carries 20–40 year useful lives; stranded assets create powerful resistance; and the populations whose livelihoods depend on fossil fuel production have legitimate grievances that price signals alone cannot address. Each framework explains something the other cannot. The technological account explains why the transition is proceeding faster than models predicted a decade ago. The political economy account explains why it is still proceeding too slowly to meet Paris Agreement targets. What determines the pace of the energy transition — economics and technology, or politics and institutional inertia? The answer implies a different theory of change, and the evidence supports both.
advanced.presentAndFuture: If Wrangham is right that fire made us human — that our cognitive complexity is downstream of a technology we adopted before we were cognitively complex — then the feedback from fire is not an accident of industrial capitalism but the consequence of what we are. And if the energy transition requires not just new technology but new political economy and new cultural assumptions about energy use, is that a problem that the species fire shaped can solve by transcending fire? This is not merely rhetorical. The question of whether behavioral and institutional change can precede the economic and technological transition, or must follow from it, is the practical policy question the confrontation points toward.

foundation.theStory: Fire is the case that most clearly exposes the limits of the "humans use tools" narrative. If Wrangham is right, fire used humans as much as humans used fire — the technology changed the species rather than merely serving it. This recursive relationship between technology and the beings who develop it is the deepest question in the history of technology, and fire is where it begins.
foundation.theDebate: The central scholarly debate is between Wrangham's cooking hypothesis — fire as the biological enabler of human cognition — and traditional accounts of fire as a tool that anatomically modern humans invented and deployed. The cooking hypothesis makes a stronger claim: fire did not just make humans more comfortable, it made humans human in a specific biological sense. The debate requires anatomy, archaeology, primatology, and evolutionary biology simultaneously.
foundation.whyItMatters: Fire matters because it is the first technology with a civilizational-scale feedback — a consequence that outlasted the intention of any individual user and eventually grew larger than the civilization that produced it. Climate change is not an industrial accident. It is the accumulated feedback of 400,000 years of hominid combustion, concentrated by 250 years of fossil fuel burning into a rate of change the atmosphere has not previously experienced.
questions: Richard Wrangham argues that cooking food was the enabling condition for Homo sapiens' brain development — meaning humans are a product of technology rather than technology being a product of humans. If he is right, what does that reversal imply about the relationship between species and the tools they develop?

Fire's most consequential feedback — climate change — was not intended by any individual whose actions produced it. What does this tell us about the relationship between individual choice and collective consequence? And who bears responsibility for outcomes no one intended?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Fire scholarship spans evolutionary biology, archaeology, anthropology, and environmental history in ways that rarely integrate cleanly. Wrangham's cooking hypothesis is primarily a biological argument taken up by archaeologists and humanists. The environmental history tradition — Stephen Pyne's body of work — focuses on fire as landscape force and civilizational technology. Comparative mythology treats fire as a cultural universal with specific symbolic functions in each tradition. Each discipline sees a different fire.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for fire's early history is fragmentary and methodologically contested. Fire leaves archaeological traces — charcoal, burnt bone, heated stone — but distinguishing controlled human fire from natural wildfire requires careful contextual analysis. The evidence for fire making versus fire use is especially difficult to establish archaeologically. Anatomical evidence — digestive tract reduction as proxy for cooking — is more reliable but depends on dating that carries its own uncertainties.
interpretation.takingAPosition: The most defensible position is that fire's relationship to human evolution is more recursive than the standard tool-use narrative captures. Hominids shaped fire; fire shaped hominids; the resulting species reshapes the planet. Understanding this circularity — rather than resolving it into a simple story of human mastery over nature — is what makes fire the right starting point for thinking seriously about technology's relationship to the species that develops it.
questions: The energy transition away from combustion is described as the first fundamental change in human energy technology since fire itself. What would make a technology transition fundamental rather than incremental — and does the current transition qualify?

Aboriginal Australians used fire as landscape management technology for tens of thousands of years. The suppression of that practice contributed to the catastrophic bushfires of 2019–20. What does this case reveal about the difference between managing fire and eliminating it?

research.topic: Archaeological evidence for early fire control
research.content: Evidence for fire use at Wonderwerk Cave (South Africa, ~1 million years ago) and Qesem Cave (Israel, ~400,000 years ago) is documented across multiple independent lines including burnt bone, charcoal, and heated stone. The distinction between controlled human fire and natural wildfire is supported by contextual analysis, though the earlier dates remain debated. Fire use by Homo erectus is accepted by most archaeologists; fire making is attributed to anatomically modern humans within the last 100,000 years.

research.topic: The carbon cycle feedback from fossil fuel combustion
research.content: The causal relationship between fossil fuel combustion and atmospheric CO₂ increase is established beyond scientific dispute. The Keeling Curve provides continuous direct measurement since 1958; ice cores extend the proxy record 800,000 years. Attribution to human combustion rather than natural variation is established through isotopic analysis: fossil carbon has a distinct carbon-14 signature identifiable in the atmospheric record.

research.topic: The cognitive consequences of fire-centered social arrangements
research.content: The hypothesis that fire created the first sustained shared attentional focus in hominid social life is consistent with Tomasello's work on shared intentionality and cumulative culture but is not directly testable from available evidence. Research in comparative primatology and developmental psychology provides indirect support for the claim that fire-maintaining behavior requires and reinforces specifically human social coordination capacities.

research.topic: The empirical status of the cooking hypothesis
research.content: The cooking hypothesis is the dominant framework in popular accounts of human evolution and has significant support from anatomical evidence — digestive tract reduction timing, dental reduction, caloric extraction comparisons between cooked and raw food. Alternative hypotheses emphasizing raw food quality improvements and meat-eating remain viable. The hypothesis is taken seriously by mainstream evolutionary biology but has not achieved full consensus.

research.topic: When and where deliberate fire making was first achieved
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: Direct archaeological evidence of fire-making tools (percussion flints, bow drill components with associated tinder materials) in securely dated contexts where natural fire sources can be excluded — establishing when humans could make fire rather than merely maintain captured fire. Why that evidence is unavailable: Fire-making tools are difficult to distinguish archaeologically from tools used for other purposes; wood and plant fiber materials used in friction fire-making do not preserve; the behavioral transition from fire use to fire making leaves no direct artifact — it is a change in procedural knowledge that material evidence cannot directly capture. What can be responsibly inferred: Universal distribution of fire-making knowledge across all modern human populations indicates fire making was established before the dispersal of anatomically modern humans out of Africa approximately 70,000 years ago. The techniques require cumulative cultural transmission, suggesting development within a socially sophisticated community. What must remain genuinely unknowable: The specific moment, location, and cognitive context of the first deliberate fire making; whether it was a single invention that spread or multiple independent inventions; and what the experience of this transition was for the humans living through it — whether recognized as a new capability or arrived through gradual improvements in fire maintenance that no individual experienced as a breakthrough.

comparativeNarrative.name: Fire as Divine Transgression
comparativeNarrative.content: The myth of Prometheus — who stole fire from the gods and gave it to humans, for which Zeus condemned him to eternal torture — is the Western tradition's foundational story about technology's costs. The myth encodes a real insight: fire's power was understood as belonging properly to a different order of being, and its human acquisition as transgressive rather than earned. Every time a major technology is described as Promethean — nuclear weapons, genetic engineering, artificial intelligence — the comparison invokes fire as the original forbidden power, asserting that some capabilities carry consequences beyond what their creators can control.

comparativeNarrative.name: Fire as Divine Intermediary
comparativeNarrative.content: In the Vedic tradition, Agni — the fire god — is the intermediary between humans and the divine. Fire in Vedic ritual is not a tool but a conduit: offerings placed in fire travel to the gods through Agni. The Agni hymns in the Rigveda are among the oldest continuously recited religious texts in human history. This understanding of fire as communication channel rather than merely energy source reflects a pre-industrial cultural reality: fire was understood as operating on a different plane than other tools, connecting the human world to something beyond it. The Vedic and Greek framings are structurally parallel despite emerging independently.

comparativeNarrative.name: Fire as Landscape Technology
comparativeNarrative.content: Aboriginal Australians developed sophisticated fire management practices over tens of thousands of years — controlled burning that maintained grasslands, reduced catastrophic wildfire risk, and maintained culturally significant plant communities. This "cultural burning" managed landscapes at a scale and refinement that European settlers, who suppressed these practices, are only now beginning to restore. The 2019–2020 Australian bushfire season — among the most destructive in recorded history — occurred in landscapes whose fire regime had been disrupted by the suppression of Aboriginal burning. Fire as ecological management technology, not just production technology, is a specifically non-Western contribution to the history of fire that the industrial tradition overlooked for two centuries.

comparativeNarrative.name: Fire as Energy Substrate
comparativeNarrative.content: The Industrial Revolution transformed fire from a local production technology into the energy substrate of an entire civilization. Steam engines, internal combustion engines, gas turbines, and coal power plants are all controlled combustion at industrial scale. The 20th century added oil and natural gas, extending industrial combustion to global atmospheric impact. The civilization that fire helped create is now confronting the necessity of dismantling its dependence on combustion — the first serious civilizational attempt to move primary energy production away from fire in the approximately 400,000-year history of controlled fire use.

rabbitHole.label: Coal
rabbitHole.reason: Coal is fire industrialized — the scale transformation of combustion into the energy substrate of modern civilization and the source of its atmospheric consequence

rabbitHole.label: Iron
rabbitHole.reason: Iron smelting requires temperatures achievable only with controlled high-heat fire — iron and fire are inseparable technologies

rabbitHole.label: Climate Change
rabbitHole.reason: Climate change is the atmospheric feedback of 400,000 years of hominid combustion concentrated into 250 years of fossil fuel burning

rabbitHole.label: Wheat
rabbitHole.reason: Wheat and fire are the two foundational technologies of agricultural civilization — wheat as stored caloric base, fire as the technology that transformed wheat into cooked food and enabled the surplus that supported cities

rabbitHole.label: Money
rabbitHole.reason: Both fire and money are foundational technologies whose most consequential effects are not what they produce but what they do to everything they touch — fire to material civilization, money to social relationships

reference.author: Richard Wrangham
reference.title: Catching Fire: How Cooking Made Us Human
reference.annotation: The cooking hypothesis in its fullest form — argues that fire applied to food was the enabling condition for Homo sapiens' brain development, inverting the standard tool-user narrative.

reference.author: Stephen Pyne
reference.title: Fire: A Brief History
reference.annotation: The most comprehensive single-volume history of fire as civilizational force — covers fire ecology, human fire management, and industrial combustion across global civilizations.

reference.author: Johan Goudsblom
reference.title: Fire and Civilization
reference.annotation: The sociological account of fire's role in civilizational development — focuses on fire's social organization rather than its biology or technology.

reference.author: Michael Tomasello
reference.title: Becoming Human: A Theory of Ontogeny
reference.annotation: The shared intentionality framework that contextualizes fire's social consequences — relevant for understanding how fire-centered arrangements may have enabled cumulative cultural evolution.

commerce.title: Catching Fire: How Cooking Made Us Human
commerce.author: Richard Wrangham
commerce.note: The essential read — makes fire's biological consequences vivid and challenges the standard picture of human cognitive supremacy

commerce.title: Fire: A Brief History
commerce.author: Stephen Pyne
commerce.note: The civilizational history — covers everything from forest fire ecology to the energy transition in a single readable volume

commerce.title: Fire and Civilization
commerce.author: Johan Goudsblom
commerce.note: The social history — how fire reorganized human societies from the Neolithic through the industrial era

popularCulture.title: Prometheus — fire as divine transgression
popularCulture.description: The Greek myth in which Prometheus steals fire from the gods — and is condemned to eternal torture for it — is the Western world's foundational story about technology and its costs. Every time a major technology is called "Promethean" (nuclear weapons, AI, genetic engineering), the comparison invokes fire as the original forbidden power: some capabilities carry consequences beyond what their creators intended or can control. The myth persists because it captures something true.

popularCulture.title: Quest for Fire (1981)
popularCulture.description: Annaud's film dramatizes competing hominid groups fighting over fire control. It remains the most serious cinematic treatment of fire's civilizational importance — and the most vivid illustration that fire was a technology worth fighting over because controlling it determined what kind of society was possible. Most technology histories understate this: some capabilities are zero-sum at the moment of their emergence.

popularCulture.title: The Olympic torch relay
popularCulture.description: The Olympic torch relay — carrying a flame from Olympia to each host city — is the world's largest ritual acknowledgment of fire's sacred status. "Carrying the torch" has entered everyday language. The ceremony descends from the ancient Greek understanding of fire as a civic and divine property rather than a physical phenomenon. It makes visible something the industrial era made invisible: fire was not always just fuel.

popularCulture.title: Australian bushfires (2019–20) and Aboriginal cultural burning
popularCulture.description: The 2019–2020 Australian bushfire season — 18 million hectares burned, an estimated 3 billion animals killed — prompted widespread discussion of Aboriginal Australian cultural burning: controlled burns that maintained landscape fire regimes for tens of thousands of years before European suppression. The disaster reframed fire management as an Indigenous knowledge problem as much as a scientific one, exposing the gap between industrial fire management and Indigenous practice.

popularCulture.title: The Keeling Curve
popularCulture.description: Keeling's continuous measurement of atmospheric CO₂ at Mauna Loa — begun in 1958 — is the most important data series in the history of fire's feedback. The curve shows a steady annual rise, passed 400 ppm in 2016, exceeds 420 ppm today. It is the most direct evidence that the feedback from controlled fire is now operating at civilizational scale — a species watching its own cumulative combustion change the atmosphere in real time.
